content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA MEDICAL ASSISTANCE COMMISSION
1121 L STREET, SUITE 300
SACRAMENTO, CA  95814
(916) 324-2726

December 12, 1998

California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Re:  Request for Advice

Dear Sir or Madam:

     In previous correspondence and conversations, the FPPC has
provided advice regarding questions I have had arising out of
my private practice of law and my duties as a member of the
California Medical Assistance Commission ("CMAC"). This is a
request for further advice.

Background Information

     The California Medical Assistance Commission consists of
seven appointed commissioners. Pursuant to statute, the
commissioners are to have backgrounds in management of hospital
services, risk management insurance/prepaid health programs,
the delivery of health services, and county health systems.
See W&I Code section 14165.1. The Commissioners serve on a part-
time basis, but have a full-time staff consisting of an
Executive Director, legal counsel, negotiators, and research
and other personnel.

     A principal purpose of CMAC is to negotiate contracts with
hospitals throughout the state. These contracts set the rates
and other terms in respect to which contracting hospitals
provide and are paid for inpatient services to Medi-Cal
recipients. In general these contracts (and amendments thereto)
are negotiated by staff and approved, rejected, or modified by
majority vote of the Commissioners at regularly scheduled
meetings. See W&I Code section 14165.5. Approximately 240
hospitals are Medi-Cal contractors and approximately 200
contracts or amendments are voted on each year by the
Commission.

     I am a health care attorney who is engaged in the private
practice of law, with my office located in Oakland,
California. The majority of my clients are involved in health
care in the San Francisco Bay Area. They include hospitals (the
bulk of which do not contract with Medi-Cal and thus have no


I cannot provide a reliable verbatim transcription of this document due to the poor legibility of the image.

Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 12, 1998
Page 2

business before CMAC), prepaid health plans, preferred provider
organizations, health care service companies, and other health
care organizations. From time to time, I have been requested
to provide legal representation to a hospital which does have a
Medi-Cal contract. In those instances where I have agreed to
provide such legal services, I have disqualified myself from
voting on or in any way participating in CMAC decisions
regarding such hospital and its contract. I have, pursuant to
the advice of CMAC legal counsel, made such disclosures as
required by pertinent provisions of the Government Code.

Specific Advice Issue

Children's Hospital Medical Center of Northern California
(Childrens Hospital-Oakland) serves many Medi-Cal eligible
children and is an important provider of inpatient services to
the Medi-Cal program. Likewise, Medi-Cal is an important source
of revenues to the hospital since a substantial portion of its
patients are Medi-Cal beneficiaries.

In late 1989, Childrens Hospital-Oakland entered into
negotiations with CMAC to, among other things, modify the
inpatient rates which it was to be paid under its existing Medi-
Cal contract. When these negotiations reached an apparent
impasse in about January 1990, I played a pro-active role in
attempting to facilitate a contract modification acceptable to
the State. In February 1990, when the hospital's contract
amendment request came before the Commission for action, I made
a motion which passed - although in a somewhat modified form.
The amendment approved by this motion was ultimately accepted
by the hospital and such concluded the matter.

Recently, Childrens Hospital-Oakland requested that I
represent the hospital as its attorney in respect to a possible
joint venture which it was discussing with a third party.
Childrens stated that its regular attorney also represented
the third party and was therefore unable to represent either
party in such a transaction.

Request For FPPC Advice

I have discussed this request with the Chairman of CMAC,
CMAC's legal counsel and Executive Director, Senate Rules
Committee staff (my appointing authority) and others. I have
requested and received the written opinion of the Chairman of
the Commission on the appropriateness of my providing such
legal representation to Childrens Hospital-Oakland. I have

Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 12, 1998
Page 3

requested and will receive the written opinion of CMAC legal
counsel regarding obligations under the Government Code.

In respect to those laws and regulations which are
administered by the Fair Political Practices Commission, it is
my understanding, based on previous discussions with and advice
from FPPC that:

* I may, consistent with FPPC rules and regulations,
provide legal services to, and receive payment from,
Childrens Hospital-Oakland provided that I disqualify
myself from voting on or participating in the
Commission's decision or action in respect to the
hospital's contract.

* Such disqualification would be during all times in
which I have a professional, compensated relationship
with the hospital and for at least one year after
such relationship ended.

If the above understanding is correct, I request that you
so advise me in writing. If my understanding of my obligations
under those laws within the FPPC's jurisdiction is incorrect,
please promptly provide me with appropriate advice.

If further information is required, please do not hesitate
to contact me at (415)451-4114. Please send your response to
me at 1970 Broadway, Suite 940, Oakland, California 94612.

I thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

ERIC P. GOLD

CC: Michael Murray,
Executive Director

Byron Chell, Esq.,
Legal Counsel

Information

The California Medical Assistance Commission consists of seven appointed commissioners. Pursuant to statute, the commissioners are to have backgrounds in management of hospital services, risk management insurance/prepaid health programs, the delivery of health services, and county health systems. See W&I Code section 14165.1. The Commissioners serve on a part-time basis, but have a full-time staff consisting of an Executive Director, legal counsel, negotiators, and research and other personnel.

A principal purpose of CMAC is to negotiate contracts with hospitals throughout the state. These contracts set the rates and other terms in respect to which contracting hospitals provide and are paid for inpatient services to Medi-Cal recipients. In general these contracts (and amendments thereto) are negotiated by staff and approved, rejected, or modified by majority vote of the Commissioners at regularly scheduled meetings. See W&I Code section 14165.5. Approximately 240 hospitals are Medi-Cal contractors and approximately 200 contracts or amendments are voted on each year by the Commission.

I am a health care attorney who is engaged in the private practice of law, with my office located in Oakland, California. The majority of my clients are involved in health care in the San Francisco Bay Area. They include hospitals (the bulk of which do not contract with Medi-Cal and thus have no

business before CMAC), prepaid health plans, preferred provider organizations, health care service companies, and other health care organizations. From time to time, I have been requested to provide legal representation to a hospital which does have a Medi-Cal contract. In those instances where I have agreed to provide such legal services, I have disqualified myself from voting on or in any way participating in CMAC decisions regarding such hospital and its contract. I have, pursuant to the advice of CMAC legal counsel, made such disclosures as required by pertinent provisions of the Government Code.

Specific Advice Issue

Children's Hospital Medical Center of Northern California (Childrens Hospital-Oakland) serves many Medi-Cal eligible children and is an important provider of inpatient services to the Medi-Cal program. Likewise, Medi-Cal is an important source of revenues to the hospital since a substantial portion of its patients are Medi-Cal beneficiaries.

In late 1989, Childrens Hospital-Oakland entered into negotiations with CMAC to, among other things, modify the inpatient rates which it was to be paid under its existing Medi-Cal contract. When these negotiations reached an apparent impasse in about January 1990, I played a pro-active role in attempting to facilitate a contract modification acceptable to the State. In February 1990, when the hospital's contract amendment request came before the Commission for action, I made a motion which passed - although in a somewhat modified form. The amendment approved by this motion was ultimately accepted by the hospital and such concluded the matter.

Recently, Childrens Hospital-Oakland requested that I represent the hospital as its attorney in respect to a possible joint venture which it was discussing with a third party. Children's stated that its regular attorney also represented the third party and was therefore unable to represent either party in such a transaction.

Request For FPPC Advice

I have discussed this request with the Chairman of CMAC, CMAC's legal counsel and Executive Director, Senate Rules Committee staff (my appointing authority) and others. I have requested and received the written opinion of the Chairman of the Commission on the appropriateness of my providing such legal representation to Childrens Hospital-Oakland. I have

Fair Political Practices Commission  
December 12, 1990  

requested and will receive the written opinion of CMAC legal counsel regarding obligations under the Government Code.

In respect to those laws and regulations which are administered by the Fair Political Practices Commission, it is my understanding, based on previous discussions with and advice from FPPC that:

* I may, consistent with FPPC rules and regulations, provide legal services to, and receive payment from, Children's Hospital-Oakland provided that I disqualify myself from voting on or participating in the Commission's decision or action in respect to the hospital's contract.

* Such disqualification would be during all times in which I have a professional, compensated relationship with the hospital and for at least one year after such relationship ended.

If the above understanding is correct, I request that you so advise me in writing. If my understanding of my obligations under those laws within the FPPC's jurisdiction is incorrect, please promptly provide me with appropriate advice.

If further information is required, please do not hesitate to contact me at (415)451-4114. Please send your response to me at 1970 Broadway, Suite 940, Oakland, California 94612.

I thank you for your attention to this matter.
